1: epeideper polloi epecheirésan anatasasthai diegésin — since many have undertaken to compile a narrative — Luke's prologue is the most literary sentence in the NT, using classical Greek historical prologues style (cf. Thucydides, Josephus). Epeideper (since indeed) is formal; anatasasthai diegesin (to draw up a narrative) uses technical historiographical vocabulary. anothen (from the beginning, from above) — Luke claims to have investigated everything carefully (parakolouthekoti, perfect participle: having followed alongside, investigated thoroughly) and writes in order (kathexes) for Theophilus to know the certainty (asphaleia) of the things taught.
46: megalynei he psyche mou ton kyrion — my soul magnifies the Lord — the Magnificat is a mosaic of OT texts, especially Hannah's prayer (1 Samuel 2:1-10). Megalynei (magnifies, declares great) is the same verb as LXX Psalms 34:3 (megazynen ton kyrion). he tapeinosis tes doules autou — the humble estate of his servant — tapeinosis (lowliness, humility) is the covenant-quality of the anawim; Mary identifies herself with the humble poor of Israel who trust YHWH's reversals. The seven reversals that follow (Luke 1:51-53) are the Magnificat's theological core: each pair presents the divine inversion of human expectation.
2: hoi ap archēs autoptai kai hyperetai genomenoi tou logou — those who from the beginning were eyewitnesses and servants of the word. Two distinct but related categories: autoptai (eyewitnesses, lit. “self-seers”) emphasizes direct visual access; hyperetai tou logou (servants of the word) designates those who transmitted the oral tradition. “The word” (logos) here is not the Johannine divine Word but the oral proclamation of the Jesus tradition — the technical term for the pre-literary gospel.
3: parekolouthēkoti anōthen pasin akribōs kathexēs soi grapsai — having carefully investigated everything from the beginning, to write an orderly account for you. Parekolouthēkoti (having followed alongside, traced carefully) combines thoroughness with personal engagement; it is a historiographical term used by Thucydides and Polybius. Kathexēs (in sequence/order) does not necessarily mean strict chronological order but thematic and narrative coherence. Luke signals his literary method: careful investigation, personal research, orderly presentation.
4: hina epignōis peri hōn katēchēthēs logōn tēn asphaleian — so that you may know the certainty of the things you have been instructed. Epignōis (full knowledge, thorough recognition) intensifies over simple gnōsis; it suggests a settled certainty rather than mere acquaintance. Katēchēthēs (instructed, catechized) is the source of the English “catechism” — Theophilus has received oral instruction, and Luke writes to ground it in documented history. Asphaleia (certainty, security) is the epistemological goal.
5: en tais hēmerais Hērōdou tou basileōs tēs Ioudaias — in the days of Herod king of Judea. Luke’s historical anchor differs from Matthew (who uses “in the days of Herod the king” without specifying Judea); the specificity is characteristic of Luke’s historiographical precision. The ephēmeria (priestly division) of Abijah places Zechariah in one of the 24 rotating priestly courses (1 Chronicles 24:10) — each serving one week twice per year.
6: dikaioi amphoteroi enantión tou theou poreuomenoi en pasais tais entolais kai dikaiōmasin tou kyriou amemptoi — both righteous in the sight of God, walking in all the commands and decrees of the Lord blamelessly. Dikaioi (righteous) is a legal-covenant term; amemptoi (blameless, without blame/fault) is its moral-character complement. The two terms together describe the complete covenant faithfulness of Zechariah and Elizabeth. They represent the best of pre-messianic Israel — faithful Torah observance awaiting the fulfillment.
7: kai ouk ēn autois teknon kathoti hē Elisabet ēn steira kai amphoteroi probebēkotes en tais hēmerais autōn ēsan — they had no child because Elizabeth was barren, and they were both advanced in their days. The barrenness-then-miraculous-birth pattern (Sarah, Rebekah, Rachel, Hannah, Samson’s mother) signals a salvation-history event: the miraculous end of barrenness always precedes a key figure in redemption. Elizabeth’s barrenness is not an accident but a narrative marker.
8: egeneto de en tō hierateuein auton en tē taxei tēs ephēmerias autou enanti tou theou — it happened that while he was serving as priest in the order of his division before God. Egeneto de is a Lukan narrative marker (Hebraism: wayehi) that introduces a new scene or event; it signals a Septuagint-influenced style. The “before God” (enanti tou theou) positions the entire scene within the divine presence — what follows happens in sacred space.
9: kata to ethos tēs hierateias elagen tou thymiasai eiselthōn eis ton naon tou kyriou — according to the custom of the priestly order, he was chosen by lot to go into the temple of the Lord and burn incense. The incense offering was the most significant individual act of priestly service (only one priest per rotation performed it); selection by lot was the method for this honor. To enter the inner sanctuary for the incense altar was the closest a non-high-priest could come to the divine presence.
10: kai pan to plēthos ēn tou laou proseuchomenon exō tō hōra tou thymiamatos — all the assembled multitude of the people were praying outside at the hour of incense. The communal prayer during the incense offering (morning and evening) is well-attested; the people stand outside the sanctuary while the priest enters. The scene establishes a double prayer: the people’s corporate prayer and Zechariah’s private prayer (Luke 1:13 — “your prayer has been heard”) whose answer arrives in the same moment.
11: ōphthē de autō angelos kyriou hestōs ek dexiōn tou thysiastēriou tou thymiamatos — an angel of the Lord appeared to him, standing at the right side of the altar of incense. Ōphthē (appeared, was seen) is the LXX term for divine appearances and post-resurrection appearances (Luke 24:34; Acts 13:31); it emphasizes the objective reality of the vision. The right side of the altar is the position of favor and power (cf. Psalms 110:1).
12: kai etarachthē Zacharias idōn kai phobos epepesen ep auton — Zechariah was startled when he saw him and fear fell upon him. Etarachthē (was troubled, agitated) and phobos epepesen (fear fell upon him) are the standard responses to theophany in the OT (Daniel 10:7-9; Judges 13:22; Isaiah 6:5). The verb epepesen (fell upon) is an idiom for sudden, overwhelming fear — not psychological anxiety but the appropriate creaturely response to numinous presence.
13: eipen de pros auton ho angelos: mē phobou Zacharia dioti eisēkousthē hē deēsis sou — but the angel said to him: Do not be afraid, Zechariah; your prayer has been heard. The standard angel-greeting mē phobou (do not be afraid) appears in every major annunciation (Luke 1:30; Luke 2:10; Daniel 10:12; Judges 6:23). Deēsis (petition, prayer of request) specifies the type of prayer — Zechariah has been praying for a child; the hearing of the prayer (divine perfect passive eisēkousthē) is announced in the moment of the angelic vision.
14: kai estai chara soi kai agalliasis — he will be a joy and delight to you. Chara (joy) and agalliasis (exultation, leaping joy) are paired throughout Luke-Acts at moments of eschatological fulfillment (Luke 1:44; Luke 10:21; Acts 2:28). Agalliasis is specifically the joy of the Psalms at divine salvation (Psalms 45:7; Luke 51:8); the arrival of John signals the beginning of eschatological joy.
15: oinos kai sikera ou mē piē kai pneumatos hagiou plēsthēsetai — he is never to take wine or strong drink, and he will be filled with the Holy Spirit. Oinos (wine) and sikera (strong drink, barley beer) are the Nazirite abstinence pair from Numbers 6:3 and the Samson narrative (Judges 13:4-5). The Nazirite dedication from birth marks John as consecrated to God’s special service. Plēsthēsetai pneumatos hagiou (will be filled with the Holy Spirit) — the filling that was characteristic of OT prophets is promised to John even from the womb (eti ek koilias mētros autou).
16: kai pollous tōn huiōn Israēl epistrepsai epi kyrion ton theon autōn — he will bring back many of the people of Israel to the Lord their God. Epistrepsai (turn back, return) is the covenant-renewal term from Malachi 3:7 (“Return to me and I will return to you”) and the great turning prophecies of the prophets. John’s mission is explicitly the turning/returning of Israel — not external reform but covenant renewal of heart (cf. Jeremiah 31:18; Ezekiel 36:26-27).
17: autos proeleusetai enōpion autou en pneumati kai dynamei Ēliou — he will go before the Lord in the spirit and power of Elijah. Enōpion autou (before him) — the pronoun refers to “the Lord their God” (Luke 1:16); John goes before YHWH, which will mean he goes before Jesus. Pneumati kai dynamei Ēliou (in the spirit and power of Elijah) — not a reincarnation but a functional correspondence: the spirit that worked through Elijah works through John for the same purpose (Malachi 4:5-6; Deuteronomy 30:6). The mission: to turn the hearts of fathers to children, and the disobedient to the wisdom of the righteous.
18: kata ti gnōsomai touto? egō gar eimi presbytēs kai hē gynē mou probebēkuia en tais hēmerais autēs — how can I know this? For I am an old man and my wife is well along in years. Zechariah’s question is formally identical to Abraham’s (Genesis 15:8 — “How can I know that I will gain possession?”) but receives a harsher answer; Abraham’s doubt was met with a covenant ceremony; Zechariah’s is met with the sign of dumbness. The difference may be the context: Abraham asked before receiving the promise; Zechariah asks after receiving it from an angelic messenger standing in the sanctuary.
19: egō eimi Gabriēl ho parestēkōs enōpion tou theou — I am Gabriel. I stand in the presence of God. Gabriel (“God is my strong one”) appears in Daniel 8:16 and Luke 9:21, where he brings prophetic revelation. His self-identification in terms of his position (“I stand in the presence of God”) invokes the heavenly court imagery of 1 Kings 22:19 and Isaiah 6:1-2 — Gabriel is one of the angelic attendants of the divine throne. The commission to “speak good news to you” (euangelisasthai) is the first use of Luke’s key gospel-proclamation verb.
20: kai idou esē siōpōn kai mē dynatos lalēsai achri hēs hēmeras genētai tauta — now you will be silent and unable to speak until the day this happens. The dumbness-as-sign (cf. Ezekiel 3:26 — “I will make your tongue stick to the roof of your mouth”) is both a judicial sign of unbelief and a prophetic sign. Zechariah will be mute until John is named (Luke 1:64), at which point his mouth opens in praise. The nine-month silence is a gestation of the word: the spoken promise is incubating in his voiceless waiting.
21: kai ēn ho laos prossdokōn ton Zacharian kai ethaumazon en tō chronizein auton en tō naō — the people were waiting for Zechariah and wondering why he stayed so long in the temple. Prosdokōn (waiting, expecting) is Luke’s characteristic word for the faithful expectation of Israel (Luke 2:25; Luke 2:38; Luke 3:15; Luke 7:19-20). The people’s waiting for Zechariah in the temple mirrors Israel’s larger waiting for the fulfillment of the covenant promises.
22: exelthōn de ouk ēdynato lalēsai autois kai epegnōsan hoti optasian heōraken en tō naō — when he came out, he could not speak to them. They realized he had seen a vision in the temple. Optasian (vision) is distinct from horama (dream-vision); it describes an objective visual appearance. The people’s recognition of the vision from Zechariah’s dumbness and gestures anticipates the sign-nature of John’s birth: something in the divine realm has happened that is visible in the ordinary world.
23: kai egeneto hōs eplēsthēsan hai hēmerai tēs leitourgias autou apēlthen eis ton oikon autou — when his time of service was completed, he returned home. Leitourgia (priestly service, liturgy) is the formal term for the temple service; Luke uses it to maintain the sacral register of the scene. Zechariah returns home after completing the week of temple service — the angelic encounter did not interrupt the service, only transformed its meaning.
24: meta de tautas tas hēmeras synelabon Elisabeth hē gynē autou kai periekryben heautēn mēnas pente — after this his wife Elizabeth became pregnant and for five months remained in seclusion. The five months of seclusion have no parallel in the OT; Elizabeth may be waiting until pregnancy is unmistakably established, or the seclusion may reflect the honor-shame dynamics of her social situation (she had been barren — a source of social reproach). The six-month marker (Luke 1:26) will bring Gabriel to Mary, connecting the two pregnancies.
25: hoti houtōs moi pepoiēken kyrios en hēmerais hais epeiden aphelesin oneidos mou en anthrōpois — the Lord has done this for me. He has shown me this kindness to remove my disgrace among the people. Oneidos (disgrace, reproach) is the word used for Rachel’s shame at barrenness (Genesis 30:23) and Hannah’s (1 Samuel 1:6); Elizabeth’s statement situates her within the great OT mothers whose barrenness was removed. The removal of disgrace is not just personal relief but a sign of divine covenant faithfulness to those who wait.
26: en de tō mēni tō hektō apestaldē ho angelos Gabriēl apo tou theou eis polin tēs Galilaias — in the sixth month God sent the angel Gabriel to Nazareth, a town in Galilee. The six-month link connects John’s and Jesus’s births into a unified divine plan; Gabriel is sent directly by God (apo tou theou), unlike his earlier appearance which was framed as a commission from his standing-presence. Galilee is identified as the geographic location: the Messiah comes from the margins of Jewish religious geography.
27: pros parthenon emnēsteumenēn andri hō onoma Iōsēph ex oikou Dauid kai to onoma tēs parthenou Mariam — to a virgin pledged to a man named Joseph, a descendant of David. Parthenos (virgin) is used twice in this verse, bracketing Mary’s identity; the emphasis on virginity anticipates the question of Luke 1:34. Ex oikou Dauid (of the house of David) modifies Joseph, establishing the legal Davidic lineage through which Jesus inherits the throne promise (2 Samuel 7:12-16; Psalms 132:11-12). Mary is not stated to be of David’s line here (though Luke 3 may imply it).
28: kecharitoménē — the single word that generated centuries of Mariology. The perfect passive participle of charitóō (to bestow grace/favor upon) means “the one who has been graced/favored” — a completed state of grace-having. The MKT “highly favoured” captures the passive (grace received, not intrinsic) and the intensive perfect (fully, completely graced). The Latin gratia plena (“full of grace,” Jerome’s Vulgate) translates the Greek idiom but has been interpreted as describing Mary’s own holiness rather than the divine action upon her.
29: hē de epi tō logō dietarachthē kai dielogizeto potapos eiē ho aspasmos houtos — Mary was greatly troubled at his word and wondered what kind of greeting this might be. Dietarachthē (deeply troubled, thoroughly agitated) is stronger than etarachthē (Luke 1:12, Zechariah’s startlement); the intensifying dia- prefix suggests Mary is more deeply moved. Her pondering (dielogizeto) is reflective and theological — she processes the greeting intellectually as well as emotionally. Luke presents Mary as one who reflects on divine revelation (cf. Luke 2:19, 51).
30: heure charin para tō theō — you have found favor with God. Charis (grace, favor) here is used as an objective divine disposition toward Mary, not a quality she possesses. The language echoes Genesis 6:8 (Noah found grace/favor in the eyes of the LORD) and Exodus 33:17 (Moses found favor with God). Finding favor is the basis for God’s mission through a person; it is divine election rather than human merit.
31: kai idou syllēpsē en gastri kai texē huion kai kaleseis to onoma autou Iēsoun — you will conceive and give birth to a son, and you are to call him Jesus. The annunciation formula (conceive-bear-name) follows the pattern of Genesis 16:11 (Hagar), Judges 13:3 (Samson’s mother), and Isaiah 7:14 (Emmanuel). Iēsous (Joshua/Jesus, “YHWH saves”) is the name whose meaning will be explained in Matthew 1:21; Luke presents the naming without the Hebrew etymology but within the annunciation pattern that evokes it.
32: houtos estai megas kai huios hypsistou klēthēsetai — he will be great and will be called Son of the Most High. The parallel with Luke 1:15 (“he will be great in the sight of the Lord” — John) is deliberate: John’s greatness is before the Lord; Jesus’s greatness is absolute (megas without qualifier). Hypsistos (Most High) is the Semitic divine title El Elyon (Genesis 14:18-20; Psalms 7:17; Daniel 4:34), used here as a circumlocution for God among Jewish audiences who avoided the divine name.
33: kai basileusei epi ton oikon Iakōb eis tous aiōnas kai tēs basileias autou ouk estai telos — he will reign over Jacob’s descendants forever; his kingdom will never end. The language is a mosaic of Davidic-covenant texts: 2 Samuel 7:13 (“his kingdom will be established forever”), Isaiah 9:7 (“of the increase of his government and peace there will be no end”), Daniel 7:14 (“his dominion is an everlasting dominion”). The eternal kingdom promised to David is fulfilled in Jesus.
34: pōs estai touto epei andra ou ginōskō? — how will this be, since I am a virgin? Mary’s question is formally similar to Zechariah’s (Luke 1:18) but different in kind: she asks about the method (how?), not the evidence (how can I know?). Andra ou ginōskō (I do not know a man) — ginōskō is the euphemism for sexual intercourse (Genesis 4:1 LXX). Her question is honest inquiry about the mechanics of fulfillment, not disbelief; she receives no rebuke.
35: pneuma hagion epeleusetai epi se kai dynamis hypsistou episkiasei soi — the Holy Spirit will come upon you, and the power of the Most High will overshadow you. Epeleusetai (will come upon) is used of the Spirit in Acts 1:8 (the Pentecost promise); it describes an empowering divine action. Episkiasei (will overshadow) is the LXX term for the cloud that covered the tabernacle (Exodus 40:35 — the glory of the LORD filled the tabernacle); the same verb appears at the Transfiguration (Luke 9:34). The conception is framed as a new creation/tabernacle event: the divine presence overshadows and life results.
36: kai idou Elisabet hē syngenēs sou kai autē syneilēphen huion en gērei autēs — even Elizabeth your relative has conceived a son in her old age. The connecting of Mary’s annunciation to Elizabeth’s miracle serves as a sign (sēmeion) that confirms the impossible; if the barren-and-aged Elizabeth has conceived, nothing is impossible with God. Syngenēs (relative, kinswoman) establishes the family connection between Jesus and John that will be narratively significant.
37: hoti ouk adynamēsei para tō theō pan rhēma — for no word from God will ever fail. The echo of Genesis 18:14 (“Is anything too hard for the LORD?”) is unmistakable. Rhēma here (word/thing/matter) differs from logos (the reasoned word) — it can mean both the spoken word and the thing/matter spoken of; the angel declares both the divine word and the divine action it announces to be beyond failure. The translation “no word from God will ever fail” captures both senses.
38: idou hē doulē kyriou genoito moi kata to rhēma sou — I am the Lord’s servant; may your word to me be fulfilled. Doulē (female slave/servant) is Mary’s self-designation; she identifies herself as the lowest-status member of the household, completely subject to the master’s will. The voluntary submission is significant: Mary’s “yes” is the model of covenant response — not merely obedience to an irresistible decree but willing submission to the divine word. Genoito (may it be, optative of wish) expresses active desire, not mere resignation.
39: anastaesa de Mariam en tais hēmerais tautais eporeuthē eis tēn oreinēn meta spoudēs — at that time Mary got ready and hurried to a town in the hill country. Anastaesa (rising up) is used for purposeful departure in Luke (cf. Luke 15:18; Acts 1:15); the urgency (meta spoudēs, with haste) reflects both her excitement and practical wisdom — she needs the confirmation of Elizabeth’s pregnancy that the angel has given as a sign. The hill country (oreinēn) of Judea is a natural geographic description connecting to the area around Hebron.
40: eisēlthen eis ton oikon Zachariou kai ēspasato tēn Elisabet — she entered Zechariah’s home and greeted Elizabeth. The greeting (aspazesthai) is the formal act of welcome that triggers the Spirit-filling; Elizabeth does not respond to Mary’s words first but to her voice (Luke 1:44). The greeting scene is structured as a Spirit-event: Mary arrives, speaks, and the Spirit-prompted response is immediate and prophetic.
41: kai egeneto hōs ēkousen ton aspasmon tēs Marias hē Elisabet eskirtēsen to brephos en tē koilia autēs — when Elizabeth heard Mary’s greeting, the baby leaped in her womb. Eskirtēsen (leaped, kicked) is the word for the joyful leaping of the psalms (Psalms 114:4,6 LXX — mountains and hills skipping at the Exodus) and of the calf released from the stall (Malachi 4:2 LXX). John’s prenatal leap is his first act of prophetic testimony to the one coming after him — the forerunner’s response begins even before birth. Pneumatos hagiou eplēsthē hē Elisabet — and Elizabeth was filled with the Holy Spirit.
42: eulogēmenē sy en gynaixin kai eulogēmenos ho karpos tēs koilias sou — blessed are you among women, and blessed is the child you will bear. Elizabeth’s blessing is a double blessing: of Mary personally and of the child she carries. The formulation echoes the blessing of Jael (Judges 5:24 LXX — “blessed among women”) and Judith (Jdt Luke 13:18); it is the highest honor-language for a woman in the Jewish tradition. The blessing of the fruit of the womb echoes Deuteronomy 28:4 (the covenant blessings).
43: kai pothen moi touto hina elthē hē mētēr tou kyriou mou pros me? — but why am I so favored, that the mother of my Lord should come to me? Hē mētēr tou kyriou mou (the mother of my Lord) — Elizabeth’s Spirit-inspired recognition of Mary’s child as kyrios (Lord) is the first human Christological confession in Luke. Kyrios in the LXX renders the divine name YHWH; Elizabeth’s use of it for the unborn child within Mary names the divine identity. This is the first instance of the “Lord” title applied to Jesus.
44: idou gar hōs egeneto hē phōnē tou aspasnou sou eis ta ōta mou eskirtēsen to brephos en agalliasei en tē koilia mou — as soon as the sound of your greeting reached my ears, the baby in my womb leaped for joy. En agalliasei (for joy, in exultation) — Elizabeth provides the interpretation of the leap that the narrator stated but did not explain: John’s prenatal leap is leaping for joy at the arrival of the Lord. The scene connects to the promise of Luke 1:14 (John will be a joy and exultation) — the promise begins to be fulfilled before his birth.
45: kai makaria hē pisteuasa hoti estai teleiōsis tois lelaleménois autē para kyriou — blessed is she who has believed that the Lord would fulfill his promises to her. The beatitude on Mary’s faith is Elizabeth’s counter to Zechariah’s unbelief (Luke 1:20): Zechariah doubted; Mary believed. Pisteuasa (having believed) is the aorist participle of completed faith; Mary’s initial “yes” (Luke 1:38) is now recognized and blessed. Teleiōsis (fulfillment, completion) is the accomplishment of the divine word — the same root as the prologue’s “things that have been fulfilled among us” (Luke 1:1).
47: kai ēgalliasen to pneuma mou epi tō theō tō sōtēri mou — and my spirit rejoices in God my Savior. Ēgalliasen (exulted, rejoiced greatly) is the Lukan joy-word from Luke 1:14 (agalliasis); Mary’s spirit leaps with the same joy that John’s body expressed in the womb. Tō sōtēri mou (my Savior) — the first use of sōtēr (Savior) in Luke; it prepares for the angel’s announcement in Luke 2:11 (“a Savior has been born to you”). Mary acknowledges her own need of salvation from the one her child will be.
48: hoti epeblepsen epi tēn tapeinōsin tēs doulēs autou idou gar apo tou nyn makariousin me pasai hai geneai — for he has been mindful of the humble state of his servant. From now on all generations will call me blessed. Tapeinōsin (lowliness, humble state) carries both social and theological meaning: Mary’s low social position (an unmarried woman of no social standing in Galilee) and her covenant self-understanding as the humble who receive divine regard. The beatitude “all generations will call me blessed” is fulfilled in the church’s memory of Mary — an eschatological prediction now in the process of fulfillment.
49: hoti epoiēsen moi megala ho dynastēs kai hagion to onoma autou — for the Mighty One has done great things for me — holy is his name. Ho dynastēs (the Mighty One, the Powerful One) is a title of divine power found in LXX Zephaniah 3:17 (“The LORD your God is in your midst, a mighty one who will save”). The “great things” (megala) echo Deuteronomy 10:21 (God did “great and awesome things” for Israel). “Holy is his name” — a liturgical formula acknowledging the divine identity as source of the salvific act.
50: kai to eleos autou eis geneas kai geneas tois phoboumenois auton — his mercy extends to those who fear him from generation to generation. Eleos (mercy, steadfast love) is the LXX rendering of Hebrew hesed (covenant faithfulness, loving-kindness); the Magnificat repeatedly uses covenant-fulfillment language. The qualification “those who fear him” defines the recipients of covenant mercy — not ethnic Israel automatically but those who stand in covenant fidelity before God.
51: epoiēsen kratos en brachioni autou dieskorpisen hyperēphanous dianoia kardias autōn — he has performed mighty deeds with his arm; he has scattered those who are proud in their inmost thoughts. The aorist verbs in Luke 1:51-55 are prophetic aorists (past tense expressing certain future fulfillment from God’s perspective). Brachioni autou (with his arm) is the Exodus arm of divine power (Psalms 98:1; Exodus 6:6). The scattering of the proud (hyperēphanous, arrogant ones) echoes 1 Samuel 2:10 (Hannah’s song) — a direct literary source for the Magnificat.
52: kathēilen dynastas apo thronōn kai hypsōsen tapeinous — he has brought down rulers from their thrones but has lifted up the humble. The eschatological reversal (rulers down, humble up) is the central theme of the Magnificat and of Luke’s Gospel broadly (cf. Luke 14:11; Luke 18:14). Dynastas (rulers, powerful ones) anticipates Herod and Caesar; the tapeinoi (humble ones) anticipate the disciples, the poor, the social outcasts who receive the kingdom. The song announces the program of what follows in the Gospel.
53: peinōntas eneplēsen agathōn kai ploutountas exapesteilen kenous — he has filled the hungry with good things but has sent the rich away empty. The dietary reversal (hungry filled, rich emptied) is Hannah’s exact language (1 Samuel 2:5 LXX). In Luke’s economy of blessing, physical hunger is a sign of spiritual readiness (the Beatitudes: “blessed are you who hunger now”, Luke 6:21) while material satiety may signal spiritual closure (“woe to you who are full now”, Luke 6:25). The Magnificat does not spiritualize poverty but treats it as a concrete reality that the kingdom reverses.
54: antelabeto Israēl paidos autou mnēsthēnai eleous — he has helped his servant Israel, remembering to be merciful. Antelabeto (has helped, has taken hold of to support) is used in the LXX for divine assistance (Isaiah 41:8-9 — God takes Israel’s hand). Paidos autou (his servant) — the servant-Israel of Isaiah 41-53 is gathered up in this phrase; Mary’s child will fulfill what Israel the servant was called to be. The covenant-memory (mnēsthēnai) frames salvation as God’s faithfulness to his own prior commitment.
55: kathōs elalēsen pros tous pateras hēmōn tō Abraam kai tō spermati autou eis ton aiōna — just as he promised to our ancestors, to Abraham and his descendants forever. The Abrahamic covenant is the ground of the Magnificat’s eschatological confidence: what God is doing in Mary’s child is the long-delayed fulfillment of what he swore to Abraham (Genesis 12:1-3; Luke 17:4-8; Luke 22:15-18). Eis ton aiōna (forever, to the age) — the covenant promises are eternal; their fulfillment in Jesus is the beginning of the eternal kingdom.
56: emeinen de Mariam syn autē hōs mēnas treis kai hypestrepsen eis ton oikon autēs — Mary stayed with Elizabeth for about three months and then returned home. The three-month stay brings Mary close to John’s birth (Elizabeth was six months pregnant when Mary arrived); Mary returns before the birth, making John’s naming and circumcision scene a separate unit. Luke’s precision (about three months) is characteristic; he uses hōs (about, approximately) for temporal estimates.
57: tē de Elisabet eplēsthē ho chronos tou tekein autēn kai egennēsen huion — when it was time for Elizabeth to have her baby, she gave birth to a son. Eplēsthē ho chronos (the time was fulfilled) is eschatological language (cf. Galatians 4:4 — “when the fullness of time came”); every birth in Luke’s infancy narrative is a fulfillment of divine timing. The birth of a son to the barren Elizabeth is the first of the two miraculous births that frame the Gospel’s opening.
58: kai ēkousan hoi perioikoi kai hoi syngeneis autēs hoti emegalynen kyrios to eleos autou met autēs kai synechairon autē — her neighbors and relatives heard that the Lord had shown her great mercy, and they shared her joy. Emegalynen to eleos (made great his mercy) — the verb echoes Mary’s Magnificat (megalynei, Luke 1:46); the greatness of God’s mercy, celebrated in Mary’s song, is now locally confirmed in Elizabeth’s neighborhood rejoicing. Synechairon (rejoiced with) is the covenant-community response to divine favor.
59: kai en tē hēmera tē ogdoē ēlthon peritemein to paidion kai ekaloun auto epi tō onomati tou patros autou Zacharian — on the eighth day they came to circumcise the child, and they were going to name him after his father Zechariah. Circumcision on the eighth day (Genesis 17:12; Leviticus 12:3) marks covenant membership; the occasion is also when the naming formally occurs in Jewish practice. The assumption that the child should be named after his father is a cultural norm; the angel’s specific naming (Luke 1:13 — “you shall call him John”) disrupts this convention.
60: kai apokritheisa hē mētēr autou eipen: ouchi alla klēthēsetai Iōannēs — his mother spoke up and said: No! He is to be called John. Elizabeth’s insistence on the God-given name, without having been present for the angelic annunciation to Zechariah, suggests her own Spirit-informed knowledge; she has been filled with the Holy Spirit (Luke 1:41) and presumably Zechariah communicated the angel’s instruction. The divine naming overrides family and cultural convention.
61: kai eipan pros autēn hoti oudeis estin ek tēs syngenias sou hos kaleitai tō onomati toutō — there is no one among your relatives who has that name. The cultural weight of family naming is strong; departing from it requires explanation. The name Iōannēs (“YHWH has been gracious”) is not unknown in the OT tradition but is not in this family’s naming lineage. The choice of a theologically significant name outside family tradition signals a break with inheritance and entrance into a new, divinely-determined identity.
62: eneneuon de tō patri autou to ti an theloi kalesthai auton — then they made signs to his father to find out what he would like to name the child. The gesture communication with Zechariah suggests he is deaf as well as mute (or they assume he cannot hear their question). The appeal to the father for the authoritative naming decision is patriarchal convention; Zechariah’s written response will confirm and seal the divine name.
63: kai aithēsamenos pinakidion egrapsen legōn: Iōannēs estin onoma autou — he asked for a writing tablet and wrote: His name is John. Pinakidion (writing tablet) — a wax tablet for everyday writing. Zechariah’s writing Iōannēs estin onoma autou (John is his name, present tense) treats the divine naming as already established fact, not a choice being made. The written declaration is the confirmation of the angelic word; his obedient act of naming releases the sign of dumbness imposed by Gabriel.
64: aneōchthē de to stoma autou parachrēma kai hē glōssa autou kai elalei eulogōn ton theon — immediately his mouth was opened and his tongue set free, and he began to speak, praising God. The instantaneous restoration (parachrēma, immediately) confirms the angelic word’s authority. Eulogōn ton theon (praising God) — Zechariah’s first words after nine months of silence are praise; the dumbness was a gestation not of bitterness but of wonder. The Benedictus that follows is the fruit of this silenced gestation.
65: kai egeneto epi pantas phobos tous perioikountas autous kai en holē tē oreinē tēs Ioudaias dielaleito panta ta rhēmata tauta — all the neighbors were filled with awe, and throughout the hill country of Judea people were talking about all these things. Phobos (awe, fear) is the numinous response to the divine presence (Luke 2:9; Luke 5:26; Luke 7:16) — not terror but reverent wonder. The spreading of “all these things” (panta ta rhēmata tauta) throughout the region anticipates the spreading of the gospel; even before Jesus’s birth, the news of his forerunner is proclaimed.
66: kai ethento pantes hoi akousantes en tē kardia autōn legontes: ti ara to paidion touto estai? — everyone who heard this wondered, saying: What then is this child going to be? The pondering response (ethento en tē kardia autōn, laid up in their hearts) mirrors Mary’s own reflective response (Luke 2:19, 51); it is the appropriate response to a divine event that exceeds immediate comprehension. The question “what then is this child going to be?” is answered by the Benedictus (prophet of the Most High, Luke 1:76) and by Jesus’s own testimony (Luke 7:28).
67: kai Zacharias ho patēr autou eplēsthē pneumatos hagiou kai eprophēteusen legōn — his father Zechariah was filled with the Holy Spirit and prophesied. The triple Spirit-filling in Luke 1 (Elizabeth, Luke 1:41; Jesus from the womb, Luke 1:15; Zechariah, Luke 1:67) establishes the Holy Spirit as the agent of the birth-narrative events. Zechariah’s prophetic utterance is identified as Spirit-speech; the Benedictus is not his personal composition but a Spirit-inspired prophetic oracle.
68: eulogētos kyrios ho theos tou Israēl hoti epeskepsato kai epoiēsen lytrōsin tō laō autou — praise be to the Lord, the God of Israel, because he has come to his people and redeemed them. Epeskepsato (visited, came to) is the covenant-visitation term (Exodus 4:31 LXX; Ruth 1:6; Psalms 106:4); when God “visits” his people, salvation follows. Lytrōsin (redemption, ransom-release) is the Exodus word for the liberation from Egypt; Zechariah’s opening declaration frames the birth of John’s Lord as a new Exodus — a liberation of God’s people from a bondage greater than Egypt.
69: kai ēgeiren keras sōtērias hēmin en oikō Dauid paidos autou — he has raised up a horn of salvation for us in the house of his servant David. Keras sōtērias (horn of salvation) is an OT idiom for a powerful deliverer (1 Samuel 2:10; Psalms 18:2; Ezekiel 29:21) — the horn being the symbol of power and strength. The horn is raised “in the house of David” — the Davidic covenant is being fulfilled. Ēgeiren (raised up) anticipates the resurrection language; the salvation-horn that is raised will be raised from death.
70: kathōs elalēsen dia stomatos tōn hagiōn ap aiōnos prophētōn autou — as he said through his holy prophets of long ago. The parenthetical verse grounds the salvation announcement in prophetic Scripture; what is happening is not new but the fulfillment of “long-standing” (ap aiōnos, from the age of ages) prophetic expectation. The entire birth narrative is framed as prophetic fulfillment.
71: sōtērian ex echthrōn hēmōn kai ek cheiros pantōn tōn misountōn hēmas — salvation from our enemies and from the hand of all who hate us. The political-liberation language of Zechariah’s Benedictus (Luke 1:71, 74) reflects the concrete hope of Israel for deliverance from Roman occupation; Jesus’s ministry will redefine “enemies” to include the spiritual powers of sin and death, and “salvation” to encompass the forgiveness (Luke 1:77) that addresses the root cause of all human bondage.
72: poiēsai eleos meta tōn paterōn hēmōn kai mnēsthēnai diathēkēs hagias autou — to show mercy to our ancestors and to remember his holy covenant. The covenant-memory of God (mnēsthēnai diathēkēs) is the covenant-faithfulness language of Psalms (Psalms 105:8; Luke 111:5); what God is doing in the birth of John’s Lord is the fulfillment of the covenantal memory he has been keeping since the patriarchs. Diathēkēs hagias (holy covenant) — the holiness of the covenant is YHWH’s own holiness pledged to its fulfillment.
73: horkon hon ōmosen pros Abraam ton patera hēmōn — the oath he swore to our father Abraham. The Abrahamic oath (Genesis 22:16-18 — sworn by himself because there was nothing greater, Hebrews 6:13-17) is the most unbreakable divine commitment in Scripture; salvation through Jesus is the oath’s fulfillment. The oath-structure of the Benedictus (God has sworn / we will serve, Luke 1:73-75) mirrors the covenant renewal structure of Deuteronomy.
74: tou dothēnai hēmin aphobōs ek cheiros echthrōn rysthentis latreuein autō — to rescue us from the hand of our enemies, and to enable us to serve him without fear. The purpose of salvation (tou dothēnai, the giving of salvation) is service: rescued from fear-based service to enemies, Israel is freed for fearless (aphobōs) service to God. The Exodus pattern recurs: liberation from Pharaoh is liberation for service to YHWH (Exodus 4:23).
75: en hosiotēti kai dikaiosynē enōpion autou pasas tas hēmeras hēmōn — in holiness and righteousness before him all our days. Hosiotēti (holiness) and dikaiosynē (righteousness) are the covenant character-pair: holiness is the vertical dimension (consecrated to God) and righteousness is the horizontal (right conduct toward others). “Before him all our days” — the life of covenant service is coram Deo (before God) — not public performance but continuous orientation toward the divine presence.
76: kai sy de paidion prophētēs hypsistou klēthēsē proporeusē gar enōpion kyriou hetoimasai hodous autou — and you, my child, will be called a prophet of the Most High; for you will go before the Lord to prepare the way for him. The transition from hymn to address to John marks the Benedictus’s second movement. Prophētēs hypsistou (prophet of the Most High) — John is “more than a prophet” (Luke 7:26) but is identified here as one; the “Most High” (hypsistos) whose prophet he is is the same Most High whose Son was just announced (Luke 1:32). Going “before the Lord” is the Malachi 3:1 role.
77: tou dounai gnōsin sōtērias tō laō autou en aphesei hamartiōn autōn — to give his people the knowledge of salvation through the forgiveness of their sins. Gnōsin sōtērias (knowledge of salvation) — John’s mission is not the full proclamation of salvation (which is Jesus’s) but the giving of knowledge of it: preparing the people to recognize and receive what Jesus brings. Aphesis hamartiōn (forgiveness of sins) is introduced here as the content of salvation; the political-liberation language of Luke 1:71-74 is now interpreted: the true enemy from which Israel needs liberation is sin.
78: dia splanchna eleous theou hēmōn en hois episkepsetai hēmas anatolē ex hypsous — because of the tender mercy of our God, by which the rising sun will come to us from heaven. Splanchna eleous (bowels/entrails of mercy) is the most visceral mercy-language in Greek — the gut-feeling of compassion (cf. Colossians 3:12; Philippians 2:1). Anatolē ex hypsous (rising/dawn from on high) — anatolē means both the rising sun and the shoot/branch (Zechariah 6:12 LXX — the Branch/Shoot prophecy of the Messiah). Both senses are active: the messianic “Branch from on high” who rises like the sun to scatter the darkness.
79: epiphanai tois en skotei kai skia thanatou kathēmenois tou kateutheynai tous podas hēmōn eis hodon eirēnēs — to shine on those living in darkness and in the shadow of death, to guide our feet into the path of peace. The dawn imagery fulfills Isaiah 9:2 (“the people walking in darkness have seen a great light”) and Isaiah 42:7 (opening blind eyes, releasing from the dungeon those who sit in darkness). Hodon eirēnēs (the path of peace) — the final word of the Benedictus; the salvation announced in Luke 1:68-77 culminates in eirēnē (shalom) — the comprehensive well-being of restored covenant relationship.
80: to de paidion ēuxanen kai ekrataiouto pneumati kai ēn en tais erēmois heōs hēmeras anadeixeōs autou pros ton Israēl — the child grew and became strong in spirit; and he lived in the wilderness until he appeared publicly to Israel. The wilderness-formation narrative of John parallels the Nazareth-formation of Jesus (Luke 2:40, 52). Erēmois (desert/wilderness) is the Elijah location (1 Kings 19:3-4) and the prophetic preparation space; John is being shaped in the desert for his desert proclamation (Luke 3:2-4). Anadeixeōs (public appearance, commissioning) is a rare word used for the installation of officials; John’s commissioning moment is the beginning of the Gospel’s main narrative.
